content.problemStatement:
Chef is the new king of the country Chefland. As first and most important responsibility he wants to reconstruct the road system of Chefland. There are **N** (**1** to **N**) cities in the country and each city **i** has a population **Pi**. Chef wants to build some bi-directional roads connecting different cities such that each city is connected to every other city (by a direct road or through some other intermediate city) and starting from any city one can visit every other city in the country through these roads. Cost of building a road between two cities **u** and **v** is **Pu x Pv**. Cost to build the road system is the sum of cost of every individual road that would be built. 

Help king Chef to find the minimum cost to build the new road system in Chefland such that every city is connected to each other.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains two lines. The first line contains an integer N, the number of cities. The second line contains N space-separated integers, the population of each city.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the minimum cost to build the new road system on a new line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, the minimum cost to build the road system is 50, which is the product of the populations of the two cities. For the second test case, the minimum cost to build the road system is 266, which is the sum of the products of the populations of the cities in the minimum spanning tree.
The minimum cost to build the road system is 700, which is the sum of the products of the populations of the cities in the minimum spanning tree.
The minimum cost to build the road system is 6, which is the sum of the products of the populations of the cities in the minimum spanning tree.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved by finding the Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) of a graph where each city is a node and the cost of building a road between two cities is the product of their populations. We can use Kruskal's algorithm to find the MST. First, we sort all possible roads in non-decreasing order of their costs. Then, we iterate over the sorted roads and add each road to the MST if it does not form a cycle with the roads already in the MST.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * (N^2 * log(N) + N * log(N))) per test case, O(T * (N^2 * log(N) + N * log(N))) total. The time complexity is dominated by the sorting of roads and the union-find operations.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N^2 + N) — the dominant structure is the array of roads and the union-find data structure.
